text: Hi, I'm Gerardo from AWS.
I'm Muci from Grab, and this is my architecture.
Today we're going to talk about the architecture of Grab Defense.
What can you tell me about this platform?
Yes, Grab Defense is a B2B service built by Grab.
We at Grab, we used to handle a lot of fraud attacks,
and we did a decent job to prevent them.
So we believe this kind of service is also needed in the market by others.
So we make this a B2B service in the AWS platform,
which can be used by others.
Brilliant, let's dive in.
So what can you tell me about the main components of the architecture?
Yes, so let me start from this, the user device here.
So Grab Defense has SDK,
which essentially collect a lot of signals from user devices,
Wi-Fi, IP address, whether this device has been emulated, spoofed.
And then we can send these events, the collect signals,
all the way to AWS manager service for Kafka,
and all the way to Neptune here,
where we can collect all the information,
put this into a graph network database, and do the later processing.
So you're streaming data into Kafka.
Why have you chosen Kafka specifically for this?
Yeah, Kafka is excellent way for us to distribute different kind of signals,
and as they are, which can be later consumed by different consumers.
Like the service here, where we're going to build a central feature bank,
and they need different signals and intelligence from the Kafka
to further consume and also build a model for this.
And how much frequency are we talking about?
How frequently are the devices pushing or streaming the data?
Yeah, so it's super high frequency.
We are pretty much processing like a couple of hundreds events per second,
and all the way to this service.
Let's talk about Neptune for now.
Why did you choose a graph database?
Yeah, graph database is excellent database for us to be able to capture
a lot of hidden patterns of the foster.
Of course, it's by nature, it's a graph.
So let's essentially we can capture, say, if these two users,
they are looks totally independent, irrelevant.
But if they share the Wi-Fi, they share the IP address or share something,
and we're able to essentially capture this through the AWS Neptune.
Okay, so what sort of queries do you make against Neptune?
Yeah, so for them, I'll say, hey, between you and me,
are we sharing any devices or are we sharing any kind of credit card?
All this kind of thing.
It's usually super hard through some regular query,
but it's super, super easy to be captured on the graph database.
That makes sense.
That makes sense.
So can you tell me about these other databases?
So you've got Redis, DynamoDB, and RDS.
What are you using them for?
Yeah, so we have different type of database.
It's mostly trying to address needs for different data type and later query patterns.
For example, DynamoDB is a good way for us to store all the user profiles,
and if we need to fetch some attributes of the users,
we can use them DB to fetch it quickly.
And also RDS.
So we have different type of events from the user,
and we want to make sure that if we need to join different events for this user,
to make sure we can get some kind of comprehensive view for user activities,
in say past X hour or something like this.
So we can use RDS to fetch data.
And last but not least, Redis, because all the things I mentioned before,
they are super complete computations.
And we use Redis for the cache purpose,
so we can retrieve the data in a super fast way.
Awesome, that makes sense.
So how are you exposing all this system to your customers?
So how can they ask questions about fraud?
Yeah, so it's pretty much here.
Our clients is here.
So later, say they have a user, they want to do a transaction.
How do we do the prediction here?
So our clients send events to us,
and all the computational service intelligence prediction service over AWS EKS.
And then I saw the EKS service,
going to also try to fetch all the data from all the central feature banks here,
along here, and we collect all the signals together,
along with the engine we have to make a comprehensive and
the non-biased decision for this particular events.
And then send the decision back to our customer.
Okay, so how much of this platform is actually multi-tenant versus
isolated or dedicated to specific customers?
Yeah, so we spend a lot of effort to make sure there are no such kind of
noisy neighbor issue for our customer.
So every customer we onboard, so they essentially have their own dedicated
database instance or DynamDB here is dedicated table to make sure, hey,
everything, so every customer, if they are running super high traffic,
the other customer wouldn't be impacted.
Along with the EKS here, where it can be easily 10x within minutes of certain
clients, they are running certain promotion and they want to 10x their traffic,
no other client is going to be impacted.
That makes sense.
What sort of scale have you achieved with this architecture?
Yeah, so we are essentially at Graph Defense, we are helping,
we are doing the prediction for millions of users and
see their day-to-day risk events there.
And also the daily log, we are at terabyte level log size.
Every day?
Yes.
